option_1_rationale: GTCS in a young child is best covered by valproate in this setting; stopping CBZ avoids relying on a focal-leaning drug when generalized epilepsy is suspected.
option_2_label: Continue carbamazepine, add valproate (bridge/overlap strategy)
option_2_rationale: If there is concern about destabilization with an abrupt switch, overlap while valproate is started/titrated, then reassess for CBZ taper once control improves.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (most available fallback)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is a highly accessible monotherapy option for GTCS, acknowledging sedation risk in severe encephalopathy.

option_1_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (preferred GTCS anchor)
option_1_rationale: For presumed primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures, valproate is the most appropriate and cost-effective first-line in this setting, and monotherapy is preferred over adding drugs.
option_2_label: If valproate cannot be used, switch to lamotrigine monotherapy
option_2_rationale: Lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative for GTCS when valproate is unsuitable, with relatively favorable cognitive profile (but requires slow titration).
option_3_label: If seizures persist despite adequate monotherapy trial, add an affordable adjunct
option_3_rationale: If valproate has been optimized yet seizures continue, clobazam is an accessible add-on option for breakthrough generalized seizures in a resource-limited setting.

option_1_label: Continue current effective dual therapy (optimize doses/adherence)
option_1_rationale: Seizure burden improved dramatically since Visit 1, so the safest plan is to continue and optimize the existing working regimen rather than switching.
option_2_label: Focal-first LMIC anchor (transition toward carbamazepine, simplify)
option_2_rationale: For focal epilepsy in this setting, carbamazepine is the cost-effective first-line anchor; consider a cautious cross-taper to monotherapy if affordability/availability is limiting and seizures are stable.
option_3_label: Accessible focal alternative if CBZ unsuitable/unavailable
option_3_rationale: Phenytoin is a widely available, low-cost focal ASM and is a reasonable fallback monotherapy if carbamazepine cannot be used or obtained.

option_1_rationale: Seizures are fairly well controlled and valproate is the most practical broad-spectrum anchor in this setting when seizure type is not specified.
option_2_label: Add low-cost adjunct if breakthrough seizures recur
option_2_rationale: If seizures are not fully controlled after ensuring valproate is at an adequate tolerated dose, clobazam is an accessible adjunct that works across seizure types without relying on knowing the exact syndrome.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital if valproate cannot be used
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is widely available/affordable in Uganda and is a reasonable fallback if valproate is unavailable, not tolerated, or becomes contraindicated.

option_1_label: Simplify to valproate + clobazam (deprescribe inducers/sedatives load)
option_1_rationale: Keeps the most appropriate broad-spectrum anchor (valproate) with one affordable adjunct (clobazam) while removing carbamazepine/phenobarbital interaction and sedation burden that may be worsening function without controlling daily seizures.
option_2_label: Valproate + levetiracetam (if sedation/tolerance is limiting or clobazam ineffective)
option_2_rationale: If ongoing daily seizures or excessive sedation on benzodiazepine/phenobarbital, switch to a cleaner non-sedating broad-spectrum adjunct (LEV) with valproate, avoiding poly-sedative therapy.
option_3_label: Phenobarbital-based minimalist fallback (if valproate not tolerated/unavailable)
option_3_rationale: In a cost/availability-constrained setting where valproate cannot be used, reverting to a single widely available ASM (phenobarbital) is a pragmatic holding strategy while reassessing seizure classification and dosing/levels.

option_1_rationale: For an infant with multifocal seizures on excessive polytherapy, keep the two most practical broad-coverage anchors (VPA+PB) and remove CBZ/TPM to reduce interactions and developmental/behavioral adverse effects while allowing true dose optimization.
option_2_label: Valproate-centered therapy with affordable adjunct (if PB problematic or seizures persist)
option_2_rationale: If phenobarbital sedation or developmental suppression is a concern, simplify to VPA plus clobazam as a pragmatic adjunct for persistent frequent seizures while avoiding mismatch drugs and polypharmacy.
option_3_label: Triple therapy after simplification (if seizures remain frequent despite Option 1 optimization)
option_3_rationale: If seizures continue at high frequency after optimizing VPA+PB doses, add clobazam as a cost-sensitive broad adjunct rather than re-adding sodium-channel–dominant CBZ or cognitively burdensome TPM.

option_1_label: Generalized/atonic-focused regimen (stop CBZ, add CLB to VPA)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best anchor for GTCS + likely atonic drop attacks; stopping carbamazepine removes a mismatched agent, and clobazam is an accessible adjunct commonly effective for drop attacks.
option_2_label: Simplify to valproate monotherapy and optimize
option_2_rationale: If valproate has not yet been titrated to an adequate dose (or adherence is uncertain), simplifying to appropriate monotherapy and optimizing it is the safest cost-effective step before committing to long-term polytherapy.
option_3_label: Add topiramate as the adjunct (if CLB not tolerated/unavailable)
option_3_rationale: Topiramate is a broad-spectrum alternative add-on for mixed generalized epilepsy with drop attacks when benzodiazepine adjunct is not feasible, acknowledging potential cognitive/language side effects in a child with developmental delay.

option_1_label: Simplify to valproate monotherapy (taper off phenobarbital)
option_1_rationale: She is seizure-free for >1 year; continuing the effective broad-spectrum anchor (valproate) while tapering phenobarbital reduces long-term sedative burden and polytherapy risk.
option_2_label: Maintain current effective dual therapy
option_2_rationale: If relapse risk is felt to be high (infant onset, possible perinatal insult) or taper monitoring is difficult, continuing the proven regimen is the safest conservative approach.
option_3_label: Transition to focal-first monotherapy with carbamazepine
option_3_rationale: If focal epilepsy is now strongly confirmed and a switch is necessary (e.g., tolerability/cost issues with current drugs), carbamazepine is the Uganda-appropriate focal anchor—though switching a seizure-free child carries relapse risk and should be done cautiously.

option_1_label: Optimize current foundational dual therapy (no new drug yet)
option_1_rationale: Both drugs are seizure-type compatible and practical in Uganda; before adding a third drug, confirm adherence/supply and optimize valproate (and PB if needed) since abrupt escalation often reflects under-dosing or missed doses.
option_2_label: Add clobazam as affordable adjunct for breakthrough seizures
option_2_rationale: If valproate/phenobarbital are already at adequate tolerated doses with good adherence and seizures persist at 2/day, clobazam is a pragmatic, accessible add-on for rapid additional control.
option_3_label: Replace phenobarbital with levetiracetam to reduce sedation burden while maintaining broad coverage
option_3_rationale: If phenobarbital is contributing to unacceptable sedation/feeding compromise or still failing despite optimization, switching PB to levetiracetam can maintain broad-spectrum control with less sedative load (accepting higher cost/availability constraints).

option_1_rationale: Seizure semiology is focal motor and CBZ is the Uganda first-line focal anchor; given improved frequency, stepping down to optimized CBZ monotherapy reduces polytherapy burden if doses/adherence can be optimized and monitored.
option_2_label: Maintain dual therapy and optimize (high-risk history)
option_2_rationale: With a history of extremely prolonged seizures, maintaining the current effective-appearing combination while optimizing dosing/adherence is a safer conservative strategy if seizure recurrence would be dangerous.
option_3_label: Switch focal anchor to phenytoin (if CBZ not tolerated/ineffective)
option_3_rationale: If CBZ is not tolerated, interacting, or not controlling seizures despite adequate dosing, PHT is an accessible focal-effective alternative while keeping VPA for broader coverage during the transition.

option_1_rationale: EEG-suggested focal epilepsy makes carbamazepine the most appropriate affordable anchor; without clear evidence of refractory seizures, simplify from triple therapy to optimized monotherapy to reduce sedation/cognitive burden and interactions.
option_2_label: Carbamazepine + clobazam dual therapy (if still having breakthrough seizures)
option_2_rationale: If seizures persist despite adequate carbamazepine dosing, retaining clobazam as the single adjunct is a practical, cost-sensitive escalation while avoiding unnecessary triple therapy.
option_3_label: Switch to valproate monotherapy (if seizure type is mixed/uncertain or CBZ failing)
option_3_rationale: If the clinical semiology is not purely focal (or carbamazepine is ineffective/intolerable), valproate provides broad-spectrum coverage in a male child and allows a simplified monotherapy regimen.

option_1_label: Optimize current GTCS regimen (confirm adherence/dose first)
option_1_rationale: Valproate is the best-matched, cost-effective anchor for generalized GTCS; with missing dose/adherence data, the first step is to ensure true optimization before adding a third drug.
option_2_label: Escalate with accessible add-on for urgent seizure clustering
option_2_rationale: With 9 seizures in 24 hours suggesting uncontrolled epilepsy, phenobarbital is a practical Uganda-available add-on that can improve control quickly when current therapy is insufficient.
option_3_label: Escalate with broad-spectrum add-on (if sedation with PB is a concern or PB unavailable)
option_3_rationale: Levetiracetam is an effective broad-spectrum adjunct with few interactions and rapid titration, used when ongoing seizures persist despite valproate+clobazam, while monitoring for possible behavioral worsening.

option_1_label: Simplify to carbamazepine monotherapy (address behavioral side effects)
option_1_rationale: Patient is seizure-free and CBZ is the focal first-line anchor in this setting; stopping LEV may improve behavioral problems while maintaining control.
option_2_label: Maintain current dual therapy while reassessing causes of behavior/cognition change
option_2_rationale: If seizure freedom is fragile or the link to LEV is unclear, continuing the working regimen is safest short-term while evaluating dose-related effects and alternative causes.
option_3_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (if CBZ not tolerated/suspected cognitive contributor)
option_3_rationale: If CBZ is contributing to cognitive slowing or other intolerance, LEV alone is a reasonable focal-effective alternative, with close monitoring for behavioral worsening.

option_1_rationale: Valproate is the preferred, cost-effective broad-spectrum ASM for idiopathic generalized epilepsy and the patient is now seizure-free on it.
option_2_label: Switch to levetiracetam monotherapy (if valproate becomes unsuitable)
option_2_rationale: If valproate cannot be continued due to intolerance, lab concerns, or access, levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum alternative that can be initiated without slow titration.
option_3_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if valproate becomes unsuitable and slow titration is acceptable)
option_3_rationale: Lamotrigine is a reasonable broad-spectrum alternative for generalized tonic-clonic seizures in idiopathic generalized epilepsy, but requires slow titration to reduce rash risk.

option_1_rationale: The child is seizure-free and the current drug is well-matched to focal seizures, so maintaining and optimizing the existing monotherapy is preferred.
option_2_label: Switch to lamotrigine monotherapy (if CBZ not tolerated/available)
option_2_rationale: LTG is an effective focal ASM with relatively favorable cognitive profile, used when CBZ cannot be continued (noting the need for slow titration due to rash risk).
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (cost/availability fallback)
option_3_rationale: PB is highly accessible and can control focal seizures in LMIC settings, used as a pragmatic fallback despite sedation/developmental concerns.

option_1_rationale: With 6 months seizure freedom and prior anger/school decline, withdrawing phenobarbital (gradually) and maintaining valproate as the GTCS-appropriate anchor minimizes cognitive/behavioral burden while preserving broad-spectrum coverage.
option_2_label: Maintain current dual therapy
option_2_rationale: If adherence is good and side effects are acceptable, continuing an effective regimen is the safest short-term strategy in a stable, seizure-free child.
option_3_label: Phenobarbital monotherapy fallback (if valproate cannot be used)
option_3_rationale: If valproate is unavailable or not tolerated, phenobarbital is an accessible monotherapy option for GTCS in this setting, though sedation/learning effects should be monitored closely.

option_1_rationale: CBZ is the first-line, cost-effective focal ASM in this setting and there is no documented breakthrough seizure pattern or intolerance to justify a change.
option_2_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (if CBZ not tolerated/unavailable)
option_2_rationale: PB is widely available/affordable in Uganda and is a reasonable focal epilepsy alternative when CBZ cannot be continued.
option_3_label: Switch to phenytoin monotherapy (alternative focal option)
option_3_rationale: PHT is an accessible focal-effective alternative if CBZ is unsuitable and PB sedation is problematic, though it carries higher long-term adverse-effect/interaction burden.

option_1_rationale: Persistent frequent focal convulsions despite CBZ+VPA suggests need for an adjunct; clobazam is a practical add-on that can rapidly reduce seizure burden without focal-type mismatch.
option_2_label: Add highly accessible adjunct (phenobarbital)
option_2_rationale: If seizures remain very frequent or clobazam is unavailable, phenobarbital is a widely available LMIC option with strong efficacy, accepting sedation/developmental tradeoffs.
option_3_label: Simplify to focal-first monotherapy strategy
option_3_rationale: If adherence/interaction burden is suspected and focal epilepsy is clear, streamlining to optimized carbamazepine monotherapy is the most guideline-consistent focal-first approach before further escalation.

option_1_rationale: The child has been seizure-free since January on valproate, so continuing the working monotherapy is preferred over switching or adding medication.
option_2_label: Switch to focal-first carbamazepine monotherapy (if valproate becomes impractical or poorly tolerated)
option_2_rationale: Carbamazepine is the Uganda baseline first-line for focal seizures and is a reasonable single-drug alternative if valproate cannot be continued for practical or side-effect reasons.
option_3_label: Switch to phenobarbital monotherapy (lowest-cost fallback if other options not feasible)
option_3_rationale: Phenobarbital is highly available and effective for focal seizures, and can be used as a cost/availability fallback despite sedation risk.

option_1_rationale: Ongoing twice-daily generalized convulsions despite CBZ+PB suggests the regimen is not appropriately matched; starting valproate (broad-spectrum, cost-effective) while continuing PB during transition offers the best chance of rapid control, with later simplification if improved.
option_2_label: Valproate monotherapy (simplify to one appropriate anchor)
option_2_rationale: If safe to transition and close follow-up is feasible, moving to valproate monotherapy prioritizes an appropriate generalized-seizure anchor and reduces sedation/interaction burden from dual older enzyme-inducing therapy.
option_3_label: If valproate is unsuitable/unavailable—phenobarbital optimization with clobazam adjunct
option_3_rationale: When valproate cannot be used, PB remains an accessible backbone and clobazam is a practical adjunct for breakthrough generalized convulsions while avoiding adding a third long-term interacting antiseizure agent.
